blocks:
Ayahs

أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يُكَذِّبُ بِٱلدِّينِ (١)
Did you see him who denies the Retribution? (1)
فَذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يَدُعُّ ٱلْيَتِيمَ (٢)
That is the one, who drives away the orphan, (2)
وَلَا يَحُضُّ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (٣)
and does not urge the feeding of the needy. (3)
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلْمُصَلِّينَ (٤)
Woe to those who pray (4)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَن صَلَاتِهِمْ سَاهُونَ (٥)
but are heedless of their prayers (5)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ يُرَآءُونَ (٦)
—who show off (6)
وَيَمْنَعُونَ ٱلْمَاعُونَ (٧)
but deny aid. (7)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Ma'un"

According to many commentators, this Surah is "Meccan," and the tone of its verses—which speak of the Resurrection and the deeds of its deniers in short, striking segments—testifies to this fact.

Altogether, the characteristics and actions of the deniers of the Resurrection are described in this Surah in five stages: how, due to their denial of this Great Day, they refuse to "spend" in the way of Allah, or help "orphans" and the "poor"; how they are negligent and hypocritical regarding "prayer"; and how they turn away from assisting the "needy."

Regarding the occasion of revelation (Sha'n al-Nuzul) of this Surah, some have said it was revealed concerning "Abu Sufyan," who used to slaughter two large camels every day for himself and his associates to consume. One day, an orphan came and asked for something; Abu Sufyan struck him with his cane and drove him away. Others have said the verse was revealed concerning "Walid ibn al-Mughirah" or "As ibn Wa'il."

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام states:

من قرأأَ رَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّينِ‌ فى فرائضه و نوافله قبل اللَّه صلاته و صيامه، و لم يحاسبه بما كان منه فى الحياة الدنيا:

"Whoever recites 'Have you seen him who denies the Recompense' in his obligatory and supererogatory prayers, Allah will accept his prayer and fasting, and He will not call him to account for what he did in the life of this world." [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 546.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

أَ رَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّينِ (1) فَذلِكَ الَّذِي يَدُعُّ الْيَتِيمَ (2) وَ لا يَحُضُّ عَلى‌ طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ (3) فَوَيْلٌ لِلْمُصَلِّينَ (4)

الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ صَلاتِهِمْ ساهُونَ (5) الَّذِينَ هُمْ يُراؤُنَ (6) وَ يَمْنَعُونَ الْماعُونَ (7)

Commentary

The Evil Effects of Denying the Hereafter

In this Surah, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is first addressed, and the evil effects of denying the Day of Recompense are recounted through the actions of the deniers.

"Have you seen him who denies the Recompense?" (أَ رَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يُكَذِّبُ بِالدِّينِ).

Then, without waiting for an answer to this question, He adds:

"For that is the one who drives away the orphan with violence!" (فَذلِكَ الَّذِي يَدُعُّ الْيَتِيمَ).

"And does not encourage others to feed the poor and the needy" (وَ لا يَحُضُّ عَلى‌ طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ).

The meaning of "Din" here is "Recompense" or the "Day of Recompense." The denial of the Day of Recompense and its Great Court has a vast impact on human actions, five of which are mentioned in this Surah, including: "driving away orphans with violence" and "not encouraging others to feed the poor." This means he neither spends himself nor invites others to do so.

Some have also suggested that "Din" here refers to the Quran or the entire religion of Islam, but the first meaning seems more appropriate. Similar usage is found in Surah Al-Infitar, verse 9: كَلَّا بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِالدِّينِ, and Surah At-Tin, verse 7: فَما يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِالدِّينِ, where, by the context of other verses in those Surahs, "Din" refers to the Day of Recompense.

"Yadu''" is derived from "Da''" (rhyming with Hadd), meaning to push away forcefully and drive away with violence and harshness.

"Yahuddu" is from "Hadd," meaning to urge and encourage others toward something. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Hass" is encouragement in movement and travel, but "Hadd" is not limited to that. [^1] Since "Yahuddu" and "Yadu''" appear in the "Mudari'" (present/continuous) verb form, it indicates that this is their persistent behavior toward orphans and the needy.

Again, a point of interest here is that regarding orphans, the issue of human emotions is emphasized more than feeding, because the greatest suffering of an orphan is the loss of an emotional center and spiritual nourishment; physical feeding comes in the second stage.

Furthermore, in these verses, we encounter the issue of feeding the "needy," which is among the most important good deeds, to the extent that it says: if one is unable to feed a needy person himself, he should encourage others to do so.

The expression "Fadhalika" (considering that the "Fa" here implies causality) points to the fact that the lack of faith in the Hereafter is the cause of these wrongdoings. Truly, he who believes in the depths of his soul in that Great Day, that Court of Justice, and that accounting, reward, and punishment, will see its positive effects manifest in all his deeds. But for those who do not believe, the effect is clearly felt in their audacity to commit sins and various crimes.

In the third description of this group, He says: "So woe to those who pray" (فَوَيْلٌ لِلْمُصَلِّينَ).

[^1]: Al-Mufradat, root "Hadd".

"Those who are heedless of their prayer" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَنْ صَلاتِهِمْ ساهُونَ).

They neither value it, nor give importance to its timings, nor observe its pillars, conditions, and etiquettes.

"Sahun" is from "Sahw," which originally means an error occurring out of negligence, whether one was at fault in preparing its preliminaries or not. Of course, in the first case, one is not excused, while in the second, one is. But here, it refers to negligence coupled with culpability.

It should be noted that He does not say "they make mistakes in their prayer," because making a mistake in prayer can happen to anyone; rather, He says "they are heedless of the prayer itself" and consign the whole of it to oblivion.

Clearly, if this happens once or a few times, it might be due to human frailty, but one who constantly forgets the prayer and consigns it to oblivion shows that he does not value it, or perhaps does not believe in it at all, and if he prays occasionally, it is out of fear of people's tongues and the like.

What is meant by "Sahun" here? In addition to what was said above, other interpretations have been given, including that it refers to delaying the prayer past its "time of virtue" (Waqt al-Fadilah).

Or that it refers to the hypocrites who believed in neither reward for prayer nor punishment for abandoning it.

Or that it refers to those who are hypocritical (Riya) in their prayers (though this meaning appears in the next verse).

Of course, combining these meanings is possible, although the first interpretation seems most appropriate.

In any case, when those who are heedless of prayer deserve "Woe," what will be the state of those who abandon prayer entirely (Tarik al-Salat)?

In the fourth stage, He points to another of their worst deeds, saying: "Those who [only] show off" (الَّذِينَ هُمْ يُراؤُنَ).

And in the final stage, He adds: "And withhold [basic] assistance" (وَ يَمْنَعُونَ الْماعُونَ).

Certainly, one of the sources of ostentation and hypocrisy is the lack of faith in the Day of Resurrection and a lack of attention to divine rewards; otherwise, how could a person abandon divine rewards and turn toward the creation and their approval? "Ma'un" is from the root "Ma'n," meaning a small thing. Many commentators believe it refers here to minor items that people, especially neighbors, borrow or take from one another, such as a bit of salt, water, fire (matches), utensils, and the like.

Obviously, someone who refuses to give such items to another is a very base and faithless person; that is, they are so stingy that they even begrudge giving these small things, while these very small things sometimes solve great needs, and withholding them creates significant problems in people's lives.

Some have also said that "Ma'un" refers to Zakat, because Zakat is usually very small compared to the original wealth—sometimes ten percent, sometimes five percent, and sometimes two and a half percent.

Of course, "withholding Zakat" is also one of the worst deeds, as Zakat solves many of the economic problems of society.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of Ma'un, he said:

هو القرض يقرضه، و المتاع يعيره، و المعروف يصنعه:

"Ma'un is the loan a person gives to another, the household items he lends to others, and the favors and good deeds a person performs." [^1] In another narration from the same Imam, this meaning is quoted, and at the end, it is mentioned that the narrator said: "We have neighbors who, when we lend them items, they break or ruin them; is it a sin if we do not give to them?"

He replied: "In that case, there is no harm." [^2] Other possibilities have been mentioned for the meaning of "Ma'un," to the extent that in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, up to twelve opinions are cited, many of which can be merged, and the most important are those we mentioned above.

Mentioning these two deeds back-to-back (hypocrisy and withholding Ma'un) seems to indicate that they perform what is for Allah with the intention of [pleasing] the creation, and they withhold from the creation what is for the creation; thus, they deliver no right to its rightful owner.

We conclude this discussion with a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who said:

من منع الماعون جاره منعه اللَّه خيره يوم القيامة، و وكله الى نفسه، و من وكله الى نفسه فما اسوء حاله؟!:

"Whoever withholds small essential items from his neighbor, Allah will withhold His goodness from him on the Day of Resurrection and leave him to himself; and whoever Allah leaves to himself, how wretched is his state?!" [^1]

Key Points

1- Summary of the Discussions in Surah Al-Ma'un

In this short Surah, a collection of vile traits is mentioned which, if found in anyone, are signs of faithlessness, baseness, and insignificance. It is noteworthy that all of them are presented as consequences of denying the Religion—that is, the Recompense or the Day of Recompense. Belittling orphans, failing to feed the hungry, negligence of prayer, hypocrisy, and lack of cooperation with people even in providing small necessities of life constitute this collection. Thus, it reflects stingy, selfish, and ostentatious individuals who have neither a bond with the "creation" nor a relationship with the "Creator"—individuals in whom the light of faith and sense of responsibility do not exist, who neither think of divine reward nor fear His punishment.

2- Ostentation and Hypocrisy: A Great Social Scourge

The value of every action depends on its "motive," or in other words, from the perspective of Islam, the foundation of every action is "intention," and specifically "pure intention."

Islam examines the record of intention before anything else. Therefore, in a famous Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

انما الاعمال بالنيات، و لكل امرء ما نوى:

"Actions are only by intentions, and every person shall have only what he intended."

In the continuation of this Hadith, it is said: "He who fights for the sake of Allah, his reward is with the Great Allah; and he who fights for the goods of this world, or even intends to obtain a 'Uqal' (a small rope used to tie a camel's leg), his share is only that." [^1] All of this is because "intention" always shapes the action. He who performs a deed for Allah builds its foundation firmly, and his entire effort is for people to benefit more from it. But he who performs a deed for ostentation and hypocrisy pays attention only to its appearance and glitter, without caring for its depth, essence, foundation, or the benefit of the needy.

A society that becomes accustomed to hypocrisy not only distances itself from Allah, good morals, and virtuous traits but also sees all its social programs emptied of content, reduced to a handful of meaningless appearances. How painful is the fate of such a person and such a society! Narrations censuring "Riya" (hypocrisy/showing off) are very numerous, to the point that it has been called a form of Shirk (polytheism). We suffice here with three striking narrations:

1- In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is said:

سياتى على الناس زمان تخبث فيه سرائرهم، و تحس فيه علانيتهم، طمعا فى الدنيا لا يريدون به ما عند ربهم، يكون دينهم رياء، لا يخالطهم خوف، يعمهم اللَّه بعقاب فيدعونه دعاء الغريق فلا يستجيب لهم!:

"A time will come upon the people when their inner selves will be foul and polluted, while their appearances will be beautiful, out of greed for the world. They do not desire what is with their Lord; their religion becomes hypocrisy, and fear of Allah does not enter their hearts. Allah will envelop them all in a severe punishment, and no matter how much they call upon Him like a drowning person, He will never answer them!" [^1]

2- In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said to one of his companions named "Zurarah":

من عمل للناس كان ثوابه على الناس يا زراره! كل رياء شرك!:

"Whoever works for the people, his reward is upon the people. O Zurarah! Every hypocrisy is Shirk!" [^2]

3- In another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is said:

ان المرائى يدعى يوم القيامة باربعة اسماء: يا كافر! يا فاجر! يا غادر! يا خاسر! حبط عملك، و بطل اجرك، فلا خلاص لك اليوم، فالتمس اجرك ممن كنت تعمل له!:

"The hypocrite will be called on the Day of Resurrection by four names: O disbeliever! O wicked one! O traitor! O loser! Your deed is nullified, and your reward is void. Today there is no escape for you; seek your reward from the one for whom you used to work!" [^3]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Riya, Hadith 14. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 1, p. 49 (under Hadith 11). [^3]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 1, p. 51 (under Hadith 16).

O Allah! Sincerity of intention is extremely difficult; help us Yourself on this path.

O Lord! Grant us such faith that we think of nothing but Your reward and punishment, and that the pleasure, satisfaction, or anger of the creation remains the same to us in Your path.

O God! Forgive us for every error we have committed on this path until now.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Ma'un.
